item.title: BMW sounds the alarm as China squeezes Europe’s carmakers
item.description: Chinese manufacturers have grown their market share in Europe while gaining share at home

item.title: A football manager gives the G7 a pep talk
item.description: Lessons from one zero-sum competition for another

item.title: What is the point of Berkshire Hathaway?
item.description: It can be bold — or become an index fund

item.title: The risks of borrowing from China’s economic playbook
item.description: Western governments must ensure industrial policy does not turn into unchecked state expansion

item.title: To avoid backlash, tech giants must share their AI wealth before it’s too late
item.description: The US cannot wait for the worst job losses to hit before moving corporate and public policy in a pro-worker direction

item.title: Tech’s private subsea cables are a threat to everyone else
item.description: By building their own data highways, American hyperscalers are creating a national security risk for other countries

item.title: Huntsman and Olin struggle to crack the formula for persuasive M&A
item.description: Mergers of equals come with execution risk

item.title: Beijing fights back against a catkin fluff invasion
item.description: The annual airborne seed attack has prompted military intervention

item.title: Europe’s emissions trading system is not working for industry
item.description: The choice is between a climate strategy that strengthens economic security and one that hollows it out

item.title: Buying Cursor could be SpaceX’s Instagram moment
item.description: Elon Musk can learn from Mark Zuckerberg by refraining from too much meddling

item.title: Bosses must master the detail without losing the vision
item.description: Paying more attention to nitty-gritty policy can help top bosses in business and politics

item.title: As stubborn Starmer digs in, Streeting risks becoming biggest loser
item.description: Former health secretary was refreshingly sharp in UK economy speech but faces a formidable set of obstacles

item.title: Three is the new two
item.description: A dispatch from the FT Global Bond Summit

item.title: Tech Exchange
item.description: This series features conversations between FT journalists and the technology industry’s leaders, innovators and thinkers. In the latest instalment, Luciana Lixandru, global co-lead of Sequoia’s early-stage investment business, talks about why it is time for ‘act two’ for Europe’s tech sector

item.title: How can we know if plants or fungi have really gone extinct?
item.description: AI is helping the Royal Botanic Gardens predict — and prevent — the demise of species

item.title: Defence not likely to be a panacea for European carmakers
item.description: An expansion into a surging industry might create buzz but it is not likely to have a big impact

item.title: The oil shortage is ending — and now comes the glut
item.description: Once flows resume properly, there will be a lot of extra oil in the system

item.title: How will a judge value my business?
item.description: I’m getting divorced and I’m really concerned about my finances

item.title: Can you keep a trade secret?
item.description: The balance between protecting ideas and shackling employees is a delicate one

item.title: We have to manage the AI revolution
item.description: There must be a global agreement on how the technology is controlled

item.title: Now is not the time to weaken the UK’s bank rules
item.description: We should be clear about who is likely to benefit from an easing in the leverage ratio

item.title: The White House’s capricious controls on Anthropic
item.description: An opaque approach to US policy risks hampering AI development

item.title: Adobe paints a vivid picture of investors’ software pessimism
item.description: Maker of creative software is hardly in crisis but it does risk losing its edge

item.title: The new German military question
item.description: France and other countries in Europe fear that Berlin is planning to go it alone

item.title: Bunzl can create enough excitement to rebut Elliott’s proposals
item.description: FTSE 100 group looks like a poor candidate for the activist treatment
